title: The Planning Bookshelf
subtitle: A curated digital library for UK planning professionals
note: Editor's picks are chosen because each is either the definitive statement of its subject or the only serious digital text covering it. Covers link to the publisher or retailer; purchases are made off the app on their site.

books.title: A Practical Approach to Planning Law
books.author: Ashley Bowes
books.reason: The current standard single-volume statement of planning law, updated to LURA 2023.
books.blurb: The 15th edition, edited by planning barrister Dr Ashley Bowes of Cornerstone Barristers and published January 2026, is the current edition of the long-running Moore & Purdue text. It works through the planning process chronologically, from whether permission is needed through applications, conditions, obligations and appeals to enforcement, and incorporates the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023.

books.title: Telling & Duxbury's Planning Law and Procedure
books.author: Robert Duxbury
books.blurb: The 16th edition (2018) remains the latest edition of this concise standard account of planning law in England and Wales, covering development, applications, enforcement, heritage, environmental assessment and court review. It is clearer and shorter than the practitioner encyclopedias and has long been a set text on RTPI-accredited and law courses.

books.title: Planning Policy
books.author: Richard Harwood OBE KC & Victoria Hutton
books.blurb: This 2018 text examines the policy framework within which planning decisions are taken: how national policy, local plans and neighbourhood plans are made, examined, interpreted and challenged. It is particularly useful to plan-making teams, examiners and advocates dealing with soundness, weight and the case law on policy interpretation.

books.title: Compulsory Purchase and Compensation
books.author: Barry Denyer-Green
books.blurb: The 12th edition (2025) of Denyer-Green's standard work explains the law of compulsory purchase and compensation in England and Wales, updated for the LURA 2023 changes including reforms bearing on hope value and the compensation code. It is the established bridge between surveying and legal practice, accessible to planners and RICS members dealing with CPOs, regeneration schemes and infrastructure consents.

books.title: Interpreting the NPPF
books.author: Alistair Mills
books.blurb: Written by Landmark Chambers barrister Alistair Mills, this 2018 book was the first in-depth commentary on the revised NPPF, analysing how the courts interpret national policy on sustainable development, the tilted balance, the Housing Delivery Test and Green Belt. Its enduring value lies in its treatment of the case law principles of policy interpretation, which continue to govern how inspectors and courts read the Framework.

books.title: Planning Permission
books.author: Richard Harwood OBE KC
books.blurb: Harwood's substantial 2016 treatise covers the whole life of a planning permission in England and Wales: the meaning of development, permitted development rights, application procedure, determination, conditions and obligations, appeals and High Court challenge. It remains the most detailed single-author treatment of development management law.

books.title: A Practical Guide to Permitted Changes of Use
books.author: Martin Goodall
books.blurb: The 4th edition (2022) of Goodall's well-known guide dissects permitted development rights for changes of use under the GPDO, fully rewritten for the 2020 Use Classes Order overhaul (Class E and Class MA) and the prior-approval regime. It is the working manual most development management officers and PD consultants actually use for prior approval and lawful development certificate work.

books.title: A Practical Guide to Planning, Highways and Development
books.author: Tom Graham
books.blurb: Tom Graham's 2019 guide covers the highway law issues that arise constantly in development management: ransom strips, access and visibility, stopping up, and Section 38 and Section 278 agreements, with annotated sample agreements. It fills a genuine gap, since highways questions sink or delay more planning applications than almost any other technical topic yet are barely covered in mainstream planning law texts.

books.title: Town and Country Planning in the UK
books.author: Barry Cullingworth, Simin Davoudi, David Webb et al.
books.reason: The definitive account of how the whole UK planning system fits together.
books.blurb: The 16th edition (2025) of 'the Cullingworth' remains the most comprehensive single account of British planning history, institutions, legislation, policy and practice, covering all four UK nations. It situates development management, plan-making, heritage, environment and infrastructure within their governance and political context in a way no legal text does.

books.title: The Short Guide to Town and Country Planning
books.author: Adam Sheppard, Nick Croft & Nick Smith
books.blurb: The second edition (2023) of this compact Policy Press guide introduces the planning systems of all UK nations, explaining how planning operates as a state activity and how governance, society and placemaking intersect. It is a low-cost orientation text well suited to new starters in LPA development management teams, planning support staff, councillors and allied professionals.

books.title: Caravans, Mobile Homes and the Planning System: A Practical Guide for UK Planners
books.author: Steve Hesmondhalgh
books.reason: The only current digital text on the caravan–planning interface, accurate to December 2025.
books.blurb: A ten-chapter reference on the awkward interface between the Caravan Sites Acts and the Town and Country Planning Act, covering statutory definitions, when siting a caravan is development, curtilage, permitted development, enforcement and certificates of lawfulness. It engages directly with the Measor principle and the Carter/Byrne line of cases, and works through seven applied scenarios from agricultural worker caravans to holiday park upgrades.

books.title: Planning Appeals: Practice and Materials
books.author: Richard Kimblin KC
books.reason: The only book dedicated to the conduct of planning appeals across all three routes.
books.blurb: Written by Richard Kimblin KC, head of the planning group at No5 Chambers, this 2020 volume is the only current book devoted specifically to the conduct of planning appeals, collating the statutory basis, procedure rules and guidance for written representations, hearings and inquiries. It gives step-by-step guidance on preparation, proofs and presentation of evidence, and examines the post-Rosewell Review inquiry regime.

books.title: Planning Enforcement
books.author: Richard Harwood OBE KC
books.blurb: The 3rd edition (2020) by Richard Harwood KC is the leading practitioner text dedicated to planning enforcement in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. It covers the full armoury of enforcement notices, stop notices, breach of condition notices, injunctions, lawful development certificates, prosecution and proceeds of crime, together with Human Rights Act constraints.

books.title: Enforcement: A Practical Guide for Planners
books.author: Steve Hesmondhalgh
books.blurb: A practitioner-focused walk-through of the complete enforcement process: expediency and discretion, breach identification, immunity and time limits, investigation, notices, appeals, injunctions, prosecution and proportionality. The text is grounded in the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 as amended by LURA 2023, and emphasises real-world decision-making over legal theory.

books.title: It's Just a Garden Room: A UK Planner's Guide to Curtilage, Incidental Use and Outbuilding Enforcement
books.author: Steve Hesmondhalgh
books.reason: Unique, case-law-based coverage of the outbuilding casework every authority faces.
books.blurb: A focused treatment of the domestic outbuilding questions that generate a disproportionate share of enforcement and lawful development casework: the legal meaning of curtilage, the 'incidental use' requirement, Class E permitted development rights, and how garden rooms drift into use as hidden dwellings or annexes. The analysis is built on case law and appeal decisions rather than bare summaries of the GPDO.

books.title: The Concise Townscape
books.author: Gordon Cullen
books.blurb: Cullen's serial-vision method of reading townscape as a sequence of unfolding views remains standard technique in UK townscape and visual impact assessment, and is cited in conservation area appraisals and design codes. The sketch-based analysis of enclosure, punctuation and focal points gives practitioners a transferable field method for character assessment.

books.title: Cities for People
books.author: Jan Gehl
books.blurb: Gehl's synthesis of five decades of public-life studies sets out a measurable, people-first methodology that directly informed UK street design guidance and healthy streets approaches. Its framework of necessary, optional and social activities gives officers and urban designers evidence-based criteria for assessing public realm proposals at eye level.

books.title: Life Between Buildings: Using Public Space
books.author: Jan Gehl
books.blurb: Gehl's foundational study of how building edges, distances and microclimate shape social activity remains the clearest primer on why some spaces are used and others stand empty. Its analysis of soft edges and the 100-metre social field gives UK designers and DM officers practical tests to apply to layouts at planning application stage.

books.title: Public Places Urban Spaces: The Dimensions of Urban Design
books.author: Matthew Carmona
books.reason: The most comprehensive synthesis of urban design knowledge in a single volume.
books.blurb: Carmona's third edition is the most comprehensive single-volume synthesis of urban design knowledge available, organised around eight substantive and process dimensions with nearly 1,000 images. Written by the UCL professor whose design governance research directly shaped English design policy, it bridges academic theory and the design-code era of UK practice.

books.title: Responsive Environments: A Manual for Designers
books.author: Ian Bentley, Sue McGlynn, Graham Smith, Alan Alcock & Paul Murrain
books.blurb: The Oxford Brookes team's step-by-step manual of permeability, variety, legibility, robustness, richness and personalisation remains the practical backbone of UK urban design appraisal and appears throughout British design guidance lineage from By Design onward. Unlike most classics it is structured as a working method, taking a site from movement framework through to detailed design decisions.

books.title: A Pattern Language: Towns, Buildings, Construction
books.author: Christopher Alexander, Sara Ishikawa & Murray Silverstein
books.blurb: Alexander's 253 interlinked patterns, from regional structure down to window seats, offer a systematic design vocabulary that continues to influence design coding, neighbourhood planning and the gentle-density movement in the UK. Its human-scale, incremental philosophy resonates with current interest in pattern books and popular design preference research.

books.title: Walkable City: How Downtown Can Save America, One Step at a Time
books.author: Jeff Speck
books.blurb: Speck distils walkability into a general theory, that the walk must be useful, safe, comfortable and interesting, backed by ten practical steps covering parking, transit, trees and street design. UK practitioners will need to translate from the American downtown context, but the economic case-making for walkable centres is directly useful in town centre strategies and active travel business cases.

books.title: The Death and Life of Great American Cities
books.author: Jane Jacobs
books.reason: The classic critique that still frames every debate on urban vitality.
books.blurb: Jacobs's 1961 attack on comprehensive redevelopment remains the most cited critique of top-down planning and underpins much of the mixed-use, fine-grain thinking now embedded in the NPPF and National Design Guide. Her concepts of mixed primary uses, short blocks, aged buildings and density concentration give UK officers a vocabulary for assessing vitality in town-centre and estate-regeneration schemes.

books.title: Urban and Regional Planning
books.author: Peter Hall & Mark Tewdwr-Jones
books.blurb: This sixth edition of Hall and Tewdwr-Jones's survey is the standard concise introduction to how urban and regional planning developed and operates in Britain, with comparative European and American material. It bridges history, institutions and contemporary governance in a way that suits conversion-course students and practitioners needing a refresher.

books.title: Happy City: Transforming Our Lives Through Urban Design
books.author: Charles Montgomery
books.blurb: Montgomery marshals behavioural science and case studies from Bogota to Vancouver to argue that urban form measurably shapes wellbeing, trust and social connection. For UK planners it provides accessible evidence for health-and-wellbeing policy justifications, engagement events and member briefings where academic texts do not land.

books.title: Cities of Tomorrow: An Intellectual History of Urban Planning and Design Since 1880
books.author: Peter Hall
books.blurb: Hall's magisterial intellectual history traces every major planning idea, from garden cities and regionalism to advocacy planning and urban renaissance, to its origins and consequences, much of it through British episodes. For UK practitioners it explains why the system and its orthodoxies took their present shape, providing essential context for policy debates from green belt to new towns.

books.title: The Purpose of Planning: Creating Sustainable Towns and Cities
books.author: Yvonne Rydin
books.blurb: Rydin's short book answers the question every planner is eventually asked, why plan at all, by setting out market-failure, collective-action and sustainability rationales in plainly written chapters grounded in the UK system. It is particularly useful for early-career officers and councillors who need a defensible articulation of planning's public value amid deregulatory pressure.

books.title: Municipal Dreams: The Rise and Fall of Council Housing
books.author: John Boughton
books.reason: The essential history behind every affordable-housing instrument in use today.
books.blurb: Boughton traces the full arc of British council housing from nineteenth-century slum clearance through the post-war building programmes to Right to Buy, stock transfer and Grenfell. It grounds current debates on affordable housing delivery, estate regeneration and viability in a rigorous estate-by-estate history rather than nostalgia.

books.title: Home Truths: The UK's Chronic Housing Shortage
books.author: Liam Halligan
books.blurb: Halligan, an economics journalist, analyses the supply-side causes of the UK housing shortage, focusing on land banking, the oligopolistic housebuilding market and the operation of the land market since the 1961 Land Compensation Act. Its treatment of land value capture and development economics is accessible and directly relevant to plan-making, CPO reform and current land-value-capture debates.

books.title: Big Capital: Who Is London For?
books.author: Anna Minton
books.blurb: Minton investigates how global capital flows, viability assessments and estate regeneration reshaped London's housing market, drawing on case studies including the Heygate and Aylesbury estates. Its critique of viability testing and affordable housing negotiation is directly relevant to Section 106 and CIL practice, and anticipated subsequent reforms to viability guidance.

books.title: All That Is Solid: How the Great Housing Disaster Defines Our Times
books.author: Danny Dorling
books.blurb: Dorling marshals extensive demographic and housing-stock data to argue that Britain's housing crisis is primarily one of distribution and under-occupation rather than simple undersupply. Some prescriptions are contested, but the statistical groundwork on tenure, wealth concentration and empty homes gives planners a counterweight to purely supply-side narratives.

books.title: Heritage Planning: Principles and Process
books.author: Harold Kalman & Marcus R. Letourneau
books.blurb: The second edition is a comprehensive, internationally comparative textbook on heritage planning practice, covering designation, significance assessment, conservation planning tools and management of change, with UK statutory systems discussed alongside North American and Australasian models. Its structured treatment of values-based assessment aligns closely with Historic England's Conservation Principles and NPPF significance tests.

books.title: Understanding Historic Building Conservation
books.author: Michael Forsyth (ed.)
books.blurb: This edited volume from the University of Bath programme combines conservation philosophy with chapters on legislation, traditional materials and the practicalities of managing change in the UK context. Contributors address topics of daily relevance to LPA casework, from conservation legislation to the behaviour of traditional construction.

books.title: Conservation of Historic Buildings
books.author: Bernard Feilden
books.blurb: Feilden's third edition remains the standard technical text on the conservation of historic structures, covering structural behaviour, causes of decay, materials, environmental control and the ethics of intervention. Written by a former surveyor of St Paul's Cathedral and director of ICCROM, it bridges conservation philosophy and buildable repair practice.

books.title: Building Adaptation
books.author: James Douglas
books.blurb: Douglas provides a systematic treatment of adapting existing buildings, extension, conversion, refurbishment and change of use, covering feasibility, structural implications, regulatory compliance and sustainability. With retrofit and adaptive reuse now central to net-zero policy and to NPPF support for making effective use of existing buildings, its technical framework is directly applicable to development management casework.

books.title: Architecture: Form, Space, and Order
books.author: Francis D. K. Ching
books.blurb: Ching's classic illustrated primer on architectural form, spatial organisation, proportion and ordering principles is now in its fifth edition with updated examples and interactive digital resources. For planners it provides a shared visual vocabulary for design review, masterplanning and drafting or applying design codes under the NPPF's design agenda.

books.title: 101 Things I Learned in Architecture School
books.author: Matthew Frederick
books.blurb: Frederick distils architectural education into 101 illustrated lessons on drawing, spatial thinking, composition and the design process. It is deliberately brief, but gives planning officers and heritage professionals without design training a quick grasp of how architects reason about site, parti and form.

books.title: Beyond the Gate: The Real Guide to Farm Diversification
books.author: Steve Hesmondhalgh
books.reason: The only current practitioner guide to farm diversification and its planning tests.
books.blurb: A 31-chapter guide to farm diversification drawing on the author's near-40 years in rural planning, covering economics, planning considerations, tourism, food enterprises, renewable energy, marketing and succession. Its distinguishing feature is the 'Diversification Toolkit', a two-level assessment framework for testing whether a proposed enterprise is actually viable before planning resources are committed.

books.title: Stand Up, Speak Out, Calm Down: A Public Speaking Guide for Planning Professionals
books.author: Steve Hesmondhalgh
books.reason: The one public-speaking guide written specifically for planning settings.
books.blurb: A skills guide addressing the specific pressures of speaking in planning contexts, committee, appeal hearings and inquiries, rather than generic corporate presentation techniques. It covers managing anxiety, structuring arguments, reading audiences, handling hostile questions and recovering from mental blocks.
